topic: A student learns their thesis advisor is leaving the university
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the library hummed a monotonous, grating tune that seemed to amplify every anxious tick in my chest. I stared at the printed email, the subject line a blunt announcement: Professor Albright is departing. My hands, slick with cold sweat, gripped the edge of my desk until my knuckles turned white. Every sentence in the announcement swam before my eyes, each word a potential catastrophe. I reread the paragraph about his planned transition, my breath catching in shallow, rapid bursts. I wanted to slam my laptop shut, to flee the echoing silence of the reading room, but my feet remained rooted to the linoleum floor. The weight of the next few months, the entire scaffolding of my research, suddenly felt impossibly precarious.

[story 2]

"It's just... a change in personnel," I managed to say, the words tasting like ash in my mouth. My voice sounded thin, reedy, completely unlike the confident tone I usually employed when discussing my data. I kept smoothing the lapel of my cardigan, a nervous habit I hadn't broken. Outside the window, the autumn rain lashed against the glass, mirroring the internal tempest brewing within me. I kept glancing at the clock; the seconds seemed to stretch into agonizing eternities. My mind raced through contingency plans, each one dissolving instantly under the sheer magnitude of the situation. I picked up my pen, intending to start outlining Chapter Three, but the ink blurred into meaningless black streaks.

[story 3]

I paced the narrow confines of my tiny apartment, the worn rug offering no comfort under my bare feet. The silence of the evening was oppressive, filled only by the frantic drumming of my own pulse. I stopped by the window, watching the distant streetlights flicker on, each one a tiny, indifferent beacon. Albright’s departure felt less like an administrative shift and more like the sudden extinguishing of a vital light source. I ran a hand through my hair, pulling at the strands until my scalp tingled unpleasantly. I slumped onto the sofa, the springs groaning under my sudden, heavy weight, and stared blankly at the ceiling, unable to summon the energy to even formulate a coherent thought about the impending void.
